refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. The refined FR focuses on the core functional need: translating mission context into measurable performance targets. This remains solution-neutral and avoids prescribing 'method,' 'document,' or 'history' which are design choices.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production schedule, safety constraints, equipment fleet composition), the system produces a traceable set of performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that satisfy the mission objectives. Verify traceability from mission inputs to derived targets.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall capture worksite mission context (site constraints, production goals, safety policies, equipment capabilities).
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall compute performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that satisfy the captured mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall establish traceability between mission context inputs and derived performance targets.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall record performance targets and their derivation rationale.
CR-NGHE-005.5: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance target baselines and calibration updates.

candidate_dps: DP-NGHE-005.1: Establish a mission-context capture mechanism that ingests worksite operational parameters (mission type, environmental conditions, safety constraints, operational policies) and maps them to a standardized performance requirement template, with version control and traceability metadata.

DP-NGHE-005.2: Implement a hierarchical performance derivation engine that translates worksite mission inputs through a rule-based or model-based inference layer to generate quantified performance targets, with explicit dependency tracking between inputs and outputs.

DP-NGHE-005.3: Design a baseline and calibration history repository that maintains immutable records of all performance target versions, associated worksite contexts, and update justifications, enabling audit trails and rollback capability.

DP-NGHE-005.4: Create a performance requirement validation and sign-off workflow that enforces traceability checks, consistency rules, and stakeholder approval gates before performance targets are committed to operational use.

potential_sources: DP-NGHE-005.1 and DP-NGHE-005.2 share dependency on a common worksite input schema; risk of schema evolution breaking both if not versioned.
DP-NGHE-005.3 (history repository) must be decoupled from DP-NGHE-005.2 (derivation engine) via a clear event/record interface to avoid tight coupling on internal state representation.
DP-NGHE-005.4 (workflow) depends on outputs from DP-NGHE-005.2 and DP-NGHE-005.3; ensure workflow logic does not embed domain rules that belong in the derivation engine.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is manageable if clear contracts (input/output schemas, event interfaces, approval state machines) are defined between DPs. Recommend explicit separation of concerns: capture (DP-005.1) → derivation (DP-005.2) → history (DP-005.3) → validation (DP-005.4), with asynchronous or event-driven integration to minimize synchronous dependencies.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite mission context is well-defined and can be captured in a structured format. Risk: Ambiguous or incomplete mission inputs may propagate through derivation, producing invalid performance targets. Mitigation: Define a mandatory worksite context schema with validation rules.

Assumption: Performance derivation rules are deterministic and can be codified (rule-based or model-based). Risk: If derivation logic is subjective or domain-expert-dependent, automation may fail or require continuous expert review. Mitigation: Validate derivation rules against historical worksite data and expert judgment; maintain a fallback manual review process.

Assumption: Traceability metadata (input → target mapping) can be automatically captured and maintained. Risk: If traceability is manual or incomplete, audit and rollback become unreliable. Mitigation: Enforce immutable logging of all derivation steps and require explicit traceability assertions before sign-off.

Assumption: Baseline and calibration history can be stored and queried efficiently. Risk: Large-scale history repositories may become slow or difficult to audit. Mitigation: Design with time-series or event-sourcing patterns; implement retention policies and archival strategies.

Assumption: Stakeholder approval workflow is feasible and timely. Risk: Workflow bottlenecks or unclear approval criteria may delay performance target deployment. Mitigation: Define clear approval roles, SLAs, and escalation paths; consider staged approval (technical → safety → operational).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-005 is well-formed and maps cleanly to the composite DP set. Recommend refining the acceptance criteria to explicitly call out: (1) schema definition and versioning for worksite mission inputs, (2) derivation rule documentation and validation methodology, (3) traceability assertion format and audit log structure, (4) approval workflow roles and decision criteria. Additionally, clarify whether 'performance targets' include both quantitative metrics (e.g., response time, accuracy) and qualitative constraints (e.g., safety margins, operational modes), and whether the system must support multi-worksite or multi-mission scenario comparison. Consider adding a sub-FR for 'performance target sensitivity analysis' to understand how changes in worksite context affect derived targets.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates remote command reception, operator input processing, vehicle state synchronization, and safety-gated actuation coordination for teleoperated vehicle control

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and vehicle telemetry streams from remote operator station

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Transmits validated motion directives and mode transitions to Vehicle control subsystem

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and hazard notifications from Safety Services; transmits operator override requests and safety state confirmations

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Publishes operator feedback, latency metrics, and control mode status to monitoring systems

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: DP-NGHE-005.1 (latency compensation), 005.2 (operator input mapping), 005.3 (state sync), and 005.4 (safety gating) are all core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert coupling to Safety Services and Tasking coupling to Vehicle are expected and well-defined.

Poor fit: Safety Services owns constraint enforcement and hazard detection, not operator command processing or latency compensation. DP-NGHE-005.2 and 005.1 would diffuse heavily into Teleoperation. DP-NGHE-005.4 (safety gating) belongs here only partially; the gating logic itself should reside in Teleoperation.

Poor fit: Vehicle owns motion execution and hardware actuation, not remote command reception or operator interaction. All four DPs would diffuse significantly; Vehicle should only receive validated Tasking directives, not implement teleoperation logic.

Very poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not a functional module. Placing DP-NGHE-005.x here would bypass architectural layering and create diffusion across all subsystems. This violates separation of concerns.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: Teleoperation subsystem (owned by remote operations or control systems team)
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The integrated composite DP (005.1 + 005.2 + 005.3 + 005.4) is cohesive within Teleoperation because all four design parameters address the same functional requirement: enabling safe, responsive remote vehicle control. Latency compensation, operator input mapping, state synchronization, and safety-gated actuation are mutually dependent and should not be split across module boundaries.
containment_risks: SafetyAlert interface coupling: Safety Services must be consulted before actuation; ensure SafetyAlert contract is well-defined to prevent tight coupling.
Network Link latency variability: Latency compensation (005.1) depends on real-time network metrics; ensure Network Link interface provides sufficient telemetry for adaptive compensation.
State sync consistency: DP-NGHE-005.3 requires bidirectional state exchange; ensure Vehicle Tasking interface supports acknowledgment and feedback to maintain sync.
Operator override logic: DP-NGHE-005.4 safety gating may require coordination with Safety Services policy; document override authority boundaries clearly.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: CRITICAL GAP: FR-NGHE-005 requires 'derive performance requirements from worksite mission context' and maintain 'documented set of performance targets traceable to worksite inputs.' The provided IM boundary (Teleoperation) and its four DPs (latency compensation, operator input mapping, state sync, safety gating) are tactical control mechanisms, NOT performance requirement derivation engines. No IM is allocated to capture, parse, or trace worksite mission context (safety plan, operational policies, environmental constraints) into performance baselines. Verification cannot be completed without an explicit IM responsible for mission-context ingestion and performance target generation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: SEVERE MISALIGNMENT: The FR explicitly demands traceability from worksite inputs to performance targets and baseline history maintenance. The DP composite (005.1-005.4) addresses remote control responsiveness and safety gating, which are implementation concerns, not requirement derivation. The Teleoperation IM is assigned responsibility for executing derived requirements, but no upstream IM owns the derivation process itself. This violates the FR->DP->IM chain: the DP does not logically satisfy the FR, and the IM does not realize the DP's intent.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-NGHE-005.1 through 005.4 are solutions to 'how to execute remote control safely and responsively,' not 'how to derive performance requirements from worksite context.' The FR demands a requirements derivation and traceability mechanism; the DP composite provides control execution tactics. This is a category error: the DP does not map to the FR's functional intent. A correct DP would specify: (1) mission context parsing (safety plan, operational policies, environmental data), (2) performance target synthesis (e.g., latency budgets, safety margins derived from worksite hazards), (3) baseline documentation and versioning, and (4) traceability linkage from worksite inputs to performance targets. The current DP set is orthogonal to this requirement.

diffusion_risks: DP-NGHE-005.1 (latency compensation) depends on real-time network telemetry; if Network Link interface does not provide sufficient fidelity or latency metrics, compensation logic will diffuse into Vehicle or Safety Services.

DP-NGHE-005.4 (safety-gated actuation) couples tightly to SafetyAlert interface; if Safety Services policy is not formally specified, gating logic will diffuse across module boundaries and create inconsistent override behavior.

DP-NGHE-005.3 (state synchronization) requires bidirectional acknowledgment from Vehicle; if Tasking interface does not support feedback, sync logic will diffuse into Vehicle control or create orphaned state in Teleoperation.

DP-NGHE-005.2 (operator input mapping) may require context-dependent remapping based on worksite mission; if mission context is not provided as a stable input, mapping logic will diffuse into Safety Services or NGHE Solution layer.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Within the scope of remote control execution, the Teleoperation IM is well-cohesive for DPs 005.1-005.4. However, this gate only evaluates the tactical control layer. The critical gap is upstream: no IM is allocated to derive the performance targets that Teleoperation will enforce. The containment score of 0.92 for Teleoperation is valid for control execution, but it masks the missing IM for requirement derivation. Recommend introducing a 'Mission Context Analyzer' or 'Performance Requirements Engine' IM upstream of Teleoperation to satisfy FR-NGHE-005's derivation mandate.

recommended_revisions: CRITICAL: Decompose FR-NGHE-005 into two distinct functional requirements: (A) FR-NGHE-005a: 'Derive performance requirements from worksite mission context' (owns mission parsing, target synthesis, baseline versioning), and (B) FR-NGHE-005b: 'Execute derived performance requirements via safe remote control' (owns latency compensation, input mapping, state sync, safety gating). Current DP composite conflates these two concerns.

Allocate a new IM 'Mission Context Analyzer' (or equivalent) to own FR-NGHE-005a. This IM must: (1) ingest worksite safety plan and operational policies as formal inputs, (2) synthesize performance targets (latency budgets, safety margins, mode constraints) traceable to worksite hazards, (3) maintain baseline history and calibration audit trail, (4) publish performance targets as a stable contract to Teleoperation IM.

Revise DP-NGHE-005 to specify the Mission Context Analyzer's design logic: e.g., 'DP-NGHE-005-MCA: Parse worksite mission context (safety plan, operational policies, environmental constraints) and synthesize performance targets (latency budgets, safety margins, mode constraints) with full traceability to source inputs and baseline versioning.'

Establish a formal interface contract between Mission Context Analyzer IM and Teleoperation IM: Mission Context Analyzer publishes 'PerformanceTargets' (direction: out) containing derived latency budgets, safety margins, and mode constraints; Teleoperation subscribes and enforces these targets in DPs 005.1-005.4.

Add acceptance criteria verification: (1) Traceability matrix linking each performance target to worksite input source (safety plan section, policy clause, environmental parameter), (2) Baseline history log with timestamps and change justifications, (3) Audit trail of calibration updates with worksite context deltas.

Document the containment risks for Teleoperation IM (SafetyAlert coupling, Network Link latency variability, state sync consistency, operator override logic) in a formal interface control document (ICD) with Safety Services and Vehicle subsystems to prevent diffusion.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-005 fails both FR-DP and FR-IM gates due to a fundamental category error: the FR demands requirement derivation and traceability from worksite mission context, but the DP composite (005.1-005.4) specifies only tactical control execution mechanisms. This is not a decomposition problem; it is a requirement misalignment that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-level decomposition. The gate evidence shows that the current DP does not logically satisfy the FR, and no IM is allocated to own the derivation process itself. Proceeding to sub-FRs without correcting this misalignment will propagate the category error through the entire decomposition tree and create unverifiable requirements at lower levels. Given high safety criticality, this gap is unacceptable.

revise.why: FR-NGHE-005 conflates two distinct functional concerns: (1) deriving performance requirements from worksite mission context (a requirements engineering function), and (2) executing those derived requirements via safe remote control (a control execution function). These must be separated into two independent FRs with distinct DPs and IMs to restore logical coherence and traceability. Attempting to decompose the current FR will only deepen the misalignment.

assumptions: Worksite mission context (safety plan, operational policies, environmental constraints) is a stable, formally documented input available at system design time or runtime.
Performance targets (latency budgets, safety margins, mode constraints) must be derived from worksite inputs, not assumed or hard-coded.
Traceability from worksite inputs to performance targets is a mandatory verification criterion for FR-NGHE-005.
The Teleoperation IM is the correct owner for remote control execution (DPs 005.1-005.4) but not for requirement derivation.
A separate IM (e.g., 'Mission Context Analyzer') is required to own the derivation process and publish performance targets as a stable contract to Teleoperation.
open_risks: If FR-NGHE-005 is not decomposed into two separate FRs, verification will fail at system integration: no evidence will exist linking performance targets to worksite mission context.

Diffusion risk: without an explicit IM for mission context parsing, the derivation logic will scatter across Safety Services, Vehicle control, and NGHE Solution layer, creating inconsistent and unmaintainable performance baselines.

Safety criticality risk: performance targets (especially latency budgets and safety margins) derived from worksite hazards are safety-critical. If derivation is not owned by a dedicated IM with formal traceability, safety assurance will be compromised.

Interface contract risk: if Mission Context Analyzer IM is not established with a formal interface to Teleoperation IM, performance targets will not propagate reliably, and Teleoperation will revert to hard-coded or ad-hoc values.

Baseline versioning risk: without a dedicated IM to maintain baseline history and audit trails, performance target changes will not be traceable to worksite context deltas, violating compliance and safety documentation requirements.
evidence_needed: Revised FR-NGHE-005a: 'Derive performance requirements from worksite mission context' with explicit acceptance criteria for traceability, baseline versioning, and audit trails.
Revised FR-NGHE-005b: 'Execute derived performance requirements via safe remote control' with DPs 005.1-005.4 mapped to this narrower functional intent.

Formal specification of Mission Context Analyzer IM: inputs (worksite safety plan, operational policies, environmental constraints), outputs (PerformanceTargets contract), and internal logic for parsing, synthesis, and versioning.
Interface control document (ICD) between Mission Context Analyzer and Teleoperation IM defining PerformanceTargets schema, update frequency, and consistency guarantees.
Traceability matrix template linking each performance target to worksite input source (safety plan section, policy clause, environmental parameter) with change justification and timestamp.

Acceptance test plan for FR-NGHE-005a: (1) parse worksite mission context from formal input, (2) synthesize performance targets with documented derivation logic, (3) maintain baseline history with audit trail, (4) verify traceability to source inputs.

Risk containment plan for Teleoperation IM diffusion risks (SafetyAlert coupling, Network Link latency variability, state sync consistency, operator override logic) with formal interface specifications to Safety Services and Vehicle subsystems.
